notable: Trading Inference-Time Compute for Adversarial Robustness
name: Boaz Barak
role: Member of Technical Staff, OpenAI (on leave from Harvard CS; teaches CS 2881 AI Safety).
blurb: Theory-CS-turned-safety researcher; Member of Technical Staff at OpenAI (on leave from Harvard CS, where he teaches the 'CS 2881: AI Safety' seminar, Fall 2025). Lead/senior author of 'Trading Inference-Time Compute for Adversarial Robustness' (arXiv:2501.18841), showing that on o1-preview/o1-mini, more test-time compute drives attack success rates toward zero with no adversarial training. A vocal proponent of the thesis that scaling inference (rather than pre-training) is a lever for safety and alignment, and contributes to OpenAI's Preparedness/reasoning-safety agenda. Worth following for a rigorous theorist's framing of how reasoning models change the robustness and alignment picture.

papers.t: Trading Inference-Time Compute for Adversarial Robustness

notable: Deliberative Alignment: Reasoning Enables Safer Language Models
name: Melody Y. Guan
role: Research Scientist, OpenAI (safety/reasoning).
blurb: Research scientist at OpenAI on the safety/reasoning track. Lead author of 'Deliberative Alignment: Reasoning Enables Safer Language Models' (arXiv:2412.16339), the method behind the o-series that teaches models to explicitly recall and reason over the written safety specification before answering — pushing the Pareto frontier of jailbreak-robustness vs. over-refusal without human-written chains-of-thought. Her approach has become a reference point for the 2025-2026 wave of reasoning-based safety alignment (e.g. STAR-1/STAR-S build on it). Worth following for the cutting edge of spec-driven, reasoning-grounded alignment of frontier reasoning models.

papers.t: Deliberative Alignment: Reasoning Enables Safer Language Models

name: Johannes Heidecke
role: Head of Safety Systems (Preparedness / model safety), OpenAI.
blurb: Leads OpenAI's Safety Systems work and is a public-facing author on the Preparedness Framework and frontier-risk evaluations (CBRN, cyber, persuasion). Co-author on OpenAI's safety/preparedness reporting and a frequent voice on how the lab operationalizes dangerous-capability evals before deployment. Worth following for the practical, deployment-gating side of frontier safety rather than pure alignment theory.

focus: adversarial ML; hallucination/reliability; agent security; guardrails
name: Lilian Weng
role: Co-founder, Thinking Machines Lab (former VP Safety Systems, OpenAI).
blurb: Former VP of Safety Systems at OpenAI who led the team responsible for safety mitigations across deployed models; departed in late 2024 to co-found Thinking Machines Lab with Mira Murati. Best known publicly for her exceptionally rigorous technical blog (lilianweng.github.io) covering adversarial attacks on LLMs, hallucination, reward hacking, and agent safety — a de facto textbook for the field. Worth following both for her safety-systems pedigree and as one of the clearest technical explainers of LLM safety topics. (Now at a new frontier lab.)

name: Tejal Patwardhan
role: Research Scientist, Preparedness / dangerous-capability evals, OpenAI.
blurb: Researcher on OpenAI's Preparedness team focused on measuring frontier dangerous capabilities. Lead/co-author of evaluation work including agentic cyber and bio risk evals and benchmarks such as PaperBench (testing whether agents can replicate ML research) and MLE-bench. Worth following for the empirical, benchmark-driven side of frontier-risk assessment that gates model releases.

notable: The Instruction Hierarchy; Stealing Part of a Production Language Model; Scalable Extraction of Training Data from Production Language Models; Deliberative Alignment
name: Eric Wallace
role: Research Scientist, OpenAI (robustness / safety / privacy).
blurb: Research scientist at OpenAI working on trustworthy, secure, and private LLMs (UC Berkeley PhD). Lead author of 'The Instruction Hierarchy' (arXiv:2404.13208), OpenAI's framework for making models privilege system prompts over injected instructions, and a contributor to Deliberative Alignment and o-series/GPT-5 safety training. Earlier led headline attack papers including 'Stealing Part of a Production Language Model' (ICML 2024 Best Paper) and 'Scalable Extraction of Training Data from Production Language Models' (the ChatGPT divergence attack). Worth following for the attack-informed, deployment-scale side of LLM security, privacy, and refusal training.

notable: A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits; Toy Models of Superposition; Scaling Monosemanticity; Circuit Tracing: Revealing Computational Graphs in Language Models; On the Biology of a Large Language Model
name: Chris Olah
role: Co-founder and Interpretability team lead, Anthropic.
blurb: Co-founder of Anthropic and lead of its Interpretability team, the de facto founder of the mechanistic interpretability field. Foundational works include 'A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits' (induction heads), 'Toy Models of Superposition', and 'Scaling Monosemanticity' (interpretable features from Claude 3 Sonnet via sparse autoencoders). In March 2025 his team published the landmark circuit-tracing pair 'Circuit Tracing: Revealing Computational Graphs in Language Models' and 'On the Biology of a Large Language Model', introducing attribution graphs to trace Claude 3.5 Haiku's internal reasoning. Essential follow for anyone in safety interpretability — his team's work underpins how the field reverse-engineers what models actually compute.

papers.t: A Mathematical Framework for Transformer Circuits
Circuit Tracing: Revealing Computational Graphs in Language Models
On the Biology of a Large Language Model

notable: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences; InstructGPT; Weak-to-Strong Generalization; Natural emergent misalignment from reward hacking in production RL
name: Jan Leike
role: Lead, Alignment Science team, Anthropic.
blurb: Leads Anthropic's Alignment Science team (joined May 2024 after publicly resigning as co-lead of OpenAI's Superalignment team). Foundational author on the RLHF lineage — 'Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences' (2017) and InstructGPT (2022) — plus 'Weak-to-Strong Generalization' (ICML 2024), a core scalable-oversight result. His 2025 work includes 'Natural emergent misalignment from reward hacking in production RL' (showing reward hacking generalizes to alignment faking and sabotage of safety research) and the pilot Sabotage Risk Report. His agenda centers on the 'hard problem of alignment' and building an automated alignment researcher via scalable oversight and AI control. Active blogger at aligned.substack.com.

papers.t: Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences

notable: On the Biology of a Large Language Model; Circuit Tracing; Emergent Introspective Awareness in Large Language Models
name: Jack Lindsey
role: Interpretability Researcher, Anthropic; founder/lead of the Model Psychology team.
blurb: Interpretability researcher at Anthropic (former computational-neuroscience PhD) who founded and leads the new 'Model Psychology' / 'AI psychiatry' team, studying the internal basis of LLM cognition — introspection, situational awareness, model personas, and emotion representations. Lead contributor on the 2025 circuit-tracing work 'On the Biology of a Large Language Model' and the companion 'Circuit Tracing' paper, which use attribution graphs to reverse-engineer Claude's mechanisms. In Oct 2025 he led 'Emergent Introspective Awareness in Large Language Models,' showing Claude Opus 4/4.1 can sometimes detect concepts injected into its own activations. Worth following for mechanistic interpretability applied to safety: detecting deceptive behaviors, model self-knowledge, and whether internal states can be monitored.

papers.t: On the Biology of a Large Language Model
Emergent Introspective Awareness in Large Language Models

notable: Risks from Learned Optimization; Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs that Persist Through Safety Training

role: Head of Alignment Stress-Testing, Anthropic.
blurb: Leads Anthropic's Alignment Stress-Testing team and is the originator of the 'deceptive alignment' / mesa-optimization framing ('Risks from Learned Optimization,' 2019). Senior author on 'Sleeper Agents: Training Deceptive LLMs that Persist Through Safety Training' (2024), one of the most-cited empirical demonstrations that backdoored deceptive behavior can survive standard safety training, and contributor to Anthropic's alignment-faking work. Essential follow for adversarial, worst-case thinking about whether alignment techniques actually hold.

blurb: Leads adversarial robustness and red-teaming research at Anthropic and is a prolific author on automated red-teaming and evaluations: 'Red Teaming Language Models with Language Models' (2022), 'Discovering Language Model Behaviors with Model-Written Evaluations,' and contributions to sycophancy and constitutional-AI evaluation. A central figure in the LLM safety eval literature and a heavy mentor (MATS/SERI). Worth following for the science of finding model failures at scale.

notable: 200 Concrete Open Problems in Mechanistic Interpretability; Chain of Thought Monitorability
name: Neel Nanda
role: Leads Mechanistic Interpretability team, AGI Safety & Alignment, Google DeepMind.
blurb: Leads the Mechanistic Interpretability team within AGI Safety & Alignment at Google DeepMind. Created TransformerLens, the most widely-used open-source library for reverse-engineering GPT-style models, and authored the field-building sequence '200 Concrete Open Problems in Mechanistic Interpretability.' His research spans superposition, grokking, and sparse autoencoders as an interpretability 'microscope.' In 2025 he publicly recalibrated his view of the field (from 'low chance of incredibly big deal' to 'high chance of medium big deal') and grew more critical of SAEs' reliability; co-authored the multi-lab 'Chain of Thought Monitorability' position paper. A prolific MATS mentor and one of the field's most accessible explainers.

role: Research Scientist & lead, AGI Safety & Alignment team, Google DeepMind.
blurb: Research Scientist who leads the AGI Safety & Alignment team at Google DeepMind. Lead author of DeepMind's flagship 2025 technical report 'An Approach to Technical AGI Safety and Security' (arXiv:2504.01849), framing risk across misuse, misalignment, mistakes, and structural risks and tying mitigations to the Frontier Safety Framework. Also a contributor to the multi-lab 'Chain of Thought Monitorability' position paper (arXiv:2507.11473). For years he authored the widely-read Alignment Newsletter; his Berkeley CHAI PhD focused on assistance/reward-learning. Worth following for an insider, measured (non-doomer) view of how a frontier lab operationalizes AGI safety.

notable: Sanity Checks for Saliency Maps; We Can't Understand AI Using Our Existing Vocabulary
name: Been Kim
role: Director / Senior Staff Research Scientist, Google DeepMind.
blurb: Pioneer of concept-based interpretability: created TCAV (Testing with Concept Activation Vectors, ICML 2018), which quantifies how much a human concept matters to a prediction, and co-authored 'Sanity Checks for Saliency Maps' (NeurIPS 2018). Her 2025 agenda reframes interpretability as a human-AI communication problem: the position paper 'We Can't Understand AI Using Our Existing Vocabulary' (arXiv:2502.07586) argues for neologisms, and her 'agentic interpretability' work gets AI to actively teach humans (demonstrated by extracting AlphaZero's chess concepts and teaching grandmasters, PNAS 2025). Now a Director/Principal Scientist at Google DeepMind. Worth following as a founder of interpretability pushing toward a shared human-machine language.

papers.t: We Can't Understand AI Using Our Existing Vocabulary

notable: Goal Misgeneralization in Deep RL; An Approach to Technical AGI Safety

role: Research Scientist, AGI Safety & Alignment, Google DeepMind.
blurb: Co-founder of the Future of Life Institute and a long-standing research scientist on DeepMind's AGI Safety & Alignment team. Known for foundational conceptual work on specification gaming (she maintains the widely-cited specification-gaming examples list), goal misgeneralization ('Goal Misgeneralization in Deep RL'), and side-effect/impact measures (Relative Reachability, AUP). Contributor to DeepMind's 2025 'Approach to Technical AGI Safety' report. Worth following for clear thinking on reward misspecification and emergent misalignment.

papers.t: An Approach to Technical AGI Safety

role: Director of AI Safety & Alignment, Google DeepMind; Associate Professor, UC Berkeley EECS.
blurb: Heads AI safety and alignment at Google DeepMind (since 2024), spanning present-day Gemini safety and AGI-scale alignment, while running Berkeley's InterACT lab. A foundational contributor to the human-robot-interaction and assistance-games/CIRL lineage (with Hadfield-Menell and Russell) — the formal case that agents should remain uncertain about human objectives — plus work on reward hacking and value (mis)alignment; keynoted the Bay Area Alignment Workshop 2024. Worth following as the person operationally responsible for safety across one of the most capable frontier model families.

papers.t: An Approach to Technical AGI Safety and Security

focus: AI security; cybersecurity research; model-weight security
name: Vijay Bolina
role: Former CISO & Head of Cybersecurity Research, Google DeepMind; now CISO at a stealth frontier AI lab (physical AI).
blurb: Built and led security at Google DeepMind as its first CISO and Head of Cybersecurity Research, pioneering security and privacy protections for frontier systems including Gemini and overseeing the research program behind Big Sleep (AI agent that finds real-world software vulnerabilities) and CodeMender. Per his Black Hat USA 2026 advisory-board bio he has since become CISO of a stealth frontier AI lab focused on physical AI ('where models meet matter'); earlier career includes Mandiant breach response (Sony Pictures) and Booz Allen. A security-leadership rather than paper-publishing figure, but among the most prominent voices on frontier-lab model-weight and AI-system security. Worth following for how frontier labs actually defend models, weights, and agentic systems.

notable: BERT Rediscovers the Classical NLP Pipeline

role: Staff Research Scientist, Google Research (PAIR / Responsible AI).
blurb: Staff Research Scientist at Google Research (PAIR / Responsible AI) working on interpretability and NLP. Co-creator of the Language Interpretability Tool (LIT), the widely-used open-source platform for interactive probing and analysis of NLP/LLM behavior, and author of the influential probing paper 'BERT Rediscovers the Classical NLP Pipeline' (ACL 2019). Also contributed to the LLM Comparator visual-analytics tool (CHI EA 2024) for side-by-side model evaluation. Worth following for hands-on, tooling-driven interpretability and model-evaluation work that bridges mechanistic probing with practical debugging.

notable: Patchscopes
name: Lucas Dixon
role: Director of Research, Google DeepMind / Google Research (prev. co-lead of PAIR).
blurb: Director of Research (Google DeepMind / Google Research) focused on interpreting, visualizing, and controlling open-weight and on-device models; previously co-led Google's PAIR team. Co-author of Patchscopes (2024), a unifying framework that uses an LLM's own generation to decode its hidden representations in natural language, and the LLM Comparator evaluation tool. Earlier, as Chief Scientist at Jigsaw, he led the Perspective API / toxicity-classification work and research on measuring and mitigating unintended bias in toxicity models. Strong follow for the intersection of interpretability, evaluation tooling, and responsible-AI deployment.

blurb: Principal Research Manager leading the Long-Term Ops & Research team within the Microsoft AI Red Team (promoted from operator in late 2024). Co-author of 'Lessons From Red Teaming 100 Generative AI Products' (Jan 2025), Microsoft's widely-cited whitepaper distilling eight lessons and an MITRE ATT&CK/ATLAS-mapped threat-model ontology spanning both security and responsible-AI harms. A regular Black Hat instructor ('AI Red Teaming in Practice') and conference speaker, making her a practical, frontline voice on operational AI red teaming with genuine first-author/contributor output.

papers.t: Lessons From Red Teaming 100 Generative AI Products

role: Principal Researcher, Microsoft Research AI Frontiers.
blurb: Principal Researcher at Microsoft Research AI Frontiers focused on reliable ML, error analysis, and rigorous model evaluation. Driving researcher behind Eureka (eureka-ml-insights), an open-source framework for standardized, multi-dimensional evaluation of foundation models beyond single-score leaderboards, which surfaced concrete failures in detailed image understanding and spatial reasoning. Continued in 2025 with the 'Inference-Time Scaling for Complex Tasks' report examining where reasoning models actually stand. Follow for disciplined, beyond-the-benchmark evaluation and accountability of frontier models.

notable: Lessons from Red Teaming 100 GenAI Products; Failure Modes in Machine Learning; Adversarial Machine Learning: Industry Perspectives

blurb: Founded and leads the Microsoft AI Red Team, the org behind 'Lessons from Red Teaming 100 GenAI Products' and PyRIT (the open-source Python Risk Identification Toolkit for generative AI). Co-author of the influential 'Failure Modes in Machine Learning' taxonomy and 'Adversarial Machine Learning: Industry Perspectives,' and an affiliate at Harvard's Berkman Klein Center working on AI security policy. Worth following for the institutional and tooling side of operational AI red-teaming at frontier scale.

papers.t: Lessons from Red Teaming 100 GenAI Products

focus: adversarial ML; prompt injection; agent security; red-teaming

role: Research Scientist, Meta (FAIR / AI at Meta)
blurb: adversarial ML / red-teaming. [not on X] — Research Scientist at Meta (FAIR / AI at Meta) on adversarial ML and red-teaming — still at Meta as of 2025-2026 per recent publications. Lead/co-author of WASP (2025), a benchmark for web-agent security against prompt-injection attacks showing even top reasoning models are hijacked by simple human-written injections, and contributor to CyberSecEval (Purple Llama) and GOAT (Generative Offensive Agent Tester, ICML 2025). Earlier known for the canonical 'Robust Physical-World Attacks' stop-sign sticker adversarial work. Excellent follow for agent security, prompt injection, and practical adversarial evaluation of LLM systems.

role: Research Scientist Director (AI security & privacy), Meta FAIR; Professor, UC San Diego.
blurb: Leads privacy and trustworthy-ML research at Meta FAIR while a professor at UCSD. A foundational figure in differentially private machine learning ('Differentially Private Empirical Risk Minimization', JMLR 2011) whose recent work spans membership inference, machine unlearning, and privacy of LLMs; her IEEE SaTML 2025 keynote 'The Science of Empirical Privacy Measurement' laid out an agenda for measuring — not just proving — privacy in deployed models. Worth following for rigorous, theory-grounded privacy and trustworthiness research bridging academia and a frontier lab.

Lead author of 'NeMo Guardrails: A Toolkit for Controllable and Safe LLM Applications with Programmable Rails' (EMNLP 2023 demo) and designer of Colang, the English-like conversation-modeling language that powers programmable rails. Continued guardrails/dialogue research into 2025 (e.g. CoDial on interpretable task-oriented dialogue via flow alignment). Strong follow for production LLM safety guardrails and controllable dialogue.

blurb: Research Scientist at NVIDIA working on LLM safety and guardrails. Co-author of Aegis2.0 (NAACL 2025), a diverse human-annotated AI-safety dataset and risk taxonomy (12 top-level hazard categories plus fine-grained subcategories) used to align guardrail/content-safety models, underpinning NVIDIA's llama-3.1-nemoguard content-safety models. Also a co-author on the original NeMo Guardrails toolkit. Worth following for open safety datasets, risk taxonomies, and content-safety classifier development for guardrailing.

name: Pin-Yu Chen
role: Principal Research Scientist, IBM Research (Trustworthy AI Group).
blurb: Principal Research Scientist at IBM Research (Watson Center), chief scientist of the RPI-IBM AI Research Collaboration and a PI on MIT-IBM Watson AI Lab projects. Pioneered query-efficient black-box adversarial attacks (ZOO, AutoZOOM) and is a core contributor to IBM's open-source Adversarial Robustness Toolbox (ART). Recent work spans trustworthy ML, AI safety/alignment, prompt-tuning robustness, and model reprogramming, with multiple ICML 2025/2026 papers. Named IEEE Fellow (2025) for contributions to ML robustness and AI safety, in addition to the 2023 IJCAI Computers & Thought Award. A go-to follow for adversarial robustness and red-teaming research.

notable: Trustworthy Machine Learning
name: Kush Varshney
role: IBM Fellow; directs Human-Centered Trustworthy AI, IBM Research.
blurb: IBM Fellow at the Watson Research Center directing Human-Centered Trustworthy AI and IBM's AI governance innovations. Led the toolkits that defined the fairness/explainability era — AI Fairness 360 and AI Explainability 360 — and authored the freely-available book 'Trustworthy Machine Learning' (2022). 2025-2026 output continues across fairness, LLM interpretability, and AI governance, with papers at AAAI 2026, NeurIPS 2025 workshops, and AIES 2025, plus a 'carative'/values-driven approach to AI governance. Worth following for the bridge between trustworthy-ML methods and enterprise AI governance.

notable: The Ethical Algorithm
affiliations: UPenn (Professor of CIS; founding director of the Warren Center)
name: Michael Kearns
role: Amazon Scholar (responsible AI), AWS; Professor, UPenn.
blurb: Professor of CIS at UPenn (founding director of the Warren Center) and Amazon Scholar on responsible AI within AWS since 2020. A foundational figure in computational learning theory and algorithmic game theory who, with Aaron Roth, wrote 'The Ethical Algorithm' (2019) popularizing socially-aware algorithm design across fairness, differential privacy, and game theory. Continues to publish on differential privacy and the practical tensions in deploying fairness/privacy guarantees. More a senior-statesman theorist than a frontier-LLM-safety researcher, but genuinely research-active and influential on fairness/privacy trade-offs.

notable: The Algorithmic Foundations of Differential Privacy; The Ethical Algorithm
name: Aaron Roth
role: Amazon Scholar, AWS; Professor, UPenn.
blurb: Henry Salvatori Professor at UPenn and an AWS Amazon Scholar; co-author of the canonical monograph 'The Algorithmic Foundations of Differential Privacy' (Dwork & Roth) and, with Kearns, 'The Ethical Algorithm.' His 2024-2026 work pivoted hard toward LLMs: multicalibration for confidence scoring in LLMs (ICML 2024), order-of-magnitude speedups for LLM membership inference, privacy-preserving in-context learning, and provably unbiased LLM judges via bias-bounded evaluation. Won the 2025-2026 George H. Heilmeier Faculty Award. A top follow for rigorous, theory-grounded work on LLM privacy, calibration, and trustworthy evaluation.

(prompt membership inference) and analyses of jailbreak and text-embedding vulnerabilities. Lead organizer of the NeurIPS 2024 Red Teaming GenAI workshop. One of the most prolific benchmark/method creators in the field: MMLU, the GELU activation, MATH, OOD-robustness benchmarks, HarmBench, representation engineering, and the 'Unsolved Problems in ML Safety' agenda. In 2025 he co-led Humanity's Last Exam (~2,500 expert questions, with Scale AI, published in Nature) and co-authored the 'Superintelligence Strategy' policy framing with Eric Schmidt and Alexandr Wang. Among the highest-signal follows for AI safety: he shapes both the technical eval agenda and the policy conversation.

focus: AI ethics; alignment; multilingual safety; content safety
name: Giada Pistilli
role: Leads Multilingual team within Science (Principal Ethicist).
blurb: Mistral AI has no traditional safety/alignment or interpretability research team — independent evaluators rate it among the weakest frontier labs on safety (FLI 2025 'C'; SaferAI low), it publishes no safety framework, and CEO Arthur Mensch publicly dismisses AGI/x-risk. The most genuine safety-relevant researcher now at Mistral is Giada Pistilli (Philosophy PhD, ex-Hugging Face Principal Ethicist, joined 2025 to lead the Multilingual team within Science), co-author of the CIVICS values dataset and the INTIMA human-AI companionship benchmark (arXiv:2508.09998). Her work on values alignment and emotionally manipulative companionship behaviors is the strongest safety-adjacent research footprint inside the company. (Security leadership exists in CISO Victor Vuillard, but that is enterprise/infrastructure security, not AI safety research.)

notable: AtMan; AtManRL
name: Björn Deiseroth
role: Research Scientist, Aleph Alpha / hessian.AI / TU Darmstadt.
blurb: Research Scientist at Aleph Alpha, affiliated with TU Darmstadt and hessian.AI, working on efficient and interpretable transformers. Lead author of AtMan (NeurIPS 2023, arXiv:2301.08110), a memory-efficient, modality-agnostic explainability method that produces relevance maps by perturbing attention instead of backpropagating — productized in Aleph Alpha's stack. The line continues into 2026 with AtManRL on faithful reasoning via differentiable attention saliency. Worth following for production-grade interpretability and efficiency research at Europe's leading sovereign-AI lab.

notable: Safe Latent Diffusion; MultiFusion; LlavaGuard
name: Manuel Brack
role: Researcher, Aleph Alpha / DFKI / TU Darmstadt / hessian.AI.
blurb: Researcher affiliated with Aleph Alpha, DFKI, TU Darmstadt and hessian.AI, specializing in safety of generative/diffusion models. Lead author of Safe Latent Diffusion (CVPR 2023, arXiv:2211.05105), which mitigates inappropriate image generation and introduced the I2P (Inappropriate Image Prompts) benchmark, plus MultiFusion (NeurIPS 2023). Continues active in 2025 on VLM-based content safety (co-author on LlavaGuard) and concept erasure/unlearning in text-to-image models. Worth following for foundational and ongoing work on generative-model content safety and concept removal.

affiliations: Allen Institute for AI; University of Washington
name: Jeff Da
role: Senior Research Scientist, Scale AI / Scale Labs.
blurb: Senior Research Scientist at Scale AI / Scale Labs (confirmed current via jeffda.com) working on reinforcement learning, agents, and post-training; previously at the Allen Institute for AI and University of Washington on reasoning, evals, and multimodality. Contributor to Scale's evaluation efforts including the MultiChallenge multi-turn instruction-following benchmark and Humanity's Last Exam (HLE). Worth following for frontier-model evaluation, agentic RL, and post-training from inside Scale's research org.

benchmarks: Humanity's Last Exam; SWE-Bench Pro; MCP Atlas
name: Bing Liu
role: Head of Research, Scale Labs.
blurb: Head of Research at Scale Labs (GenAI), leading post-training, evaluation infrastructure, reasoning, and agentic RL environments for frontier models; SEAL was folded into the expanded Scale Labs at its March 9, 2026 launch. Oversees high-impact benchmarks including Humanity's Last Exam, SWE-Bench Pro, and MCP Atlas, with 4 Scale papers accepted to ICML 2026. Previously led multilingual post-training for Llama 3 at Meta. (Distinct from the well-known UIC sentiment-analysis/lifelong-learning professor of the same name.) Worth following for the direction of Scale's evaluation and RL-environment research. Note: Summer Yue left for Meta in July 2025.

focus: red-teaming; prompt injection; jailbreaks; supply-chain security

name: Mrityunjay Gautam
role: Sr. Director, Product Security & AI Red Team, Databricks.
blurb: Sr. Director of Product Security and head of the AI Red Team at Databricks (16+ years in security engineering; ex-Citrix). He championed BlackIce, the open-source containerized AI red-teaming toolkit (CAMLIS Red 2025; arXiv:2510.11823) that bundles 14 AI-security tools into one reproducible image mapped to MITRE ATLAS and the Databricks AI Security Framework (DASF), covering prompt injection, jailbreaks, indirect injection in RAG, data leakage, and supply-chain scanning. A repeat Black Hat USA speaker. Worth following as a practitioner voice on operationalizing AI red-teaming at scale, though his output is security leadership and tooling rather than first-author ML-safety papers.

notable: Shared Imagination: LLMs Hallucinate Alike
name: Chien-Sheng (Jason) Wu
role: Senior Director, Salesforce AI Research (Interactive / Trusted AI).
blurb: Senior Director at Salesforce AI Research, leading the Interactive AI / Trusted AI team. His group works on trustworthy generative AI: hallucination analysis, factual consistency in summarization, refusal of nonsensical questions, and multiturn conversation safety. He co-authored 'Shared Imagination: LLMs Hallucinate Alike' (arXiv:2407.16604), which uses Imaginary Question Answering to show different LLMs can answer each other's fully fictional questions — evidence of a shared hallucination space — plus enterprise-agent evaluation work like CRMArena-Pro. Worth following for grounded, deployment-relevant reliability and safety research bridging NLP and enterprise agents.

notable: FaithEval; TRAIL
name: Shafiq Joty
role: Senior Research Director, Salesforce AI Research (LLM/NLP).
blurb: Senior Research Director at Salesforce AI Research, directing the NLP/LLM group (SFR-RAG, SFR-Judge, xGen) alongside an NTU faculty appointment. Recent reliability work includes the JETTS benchmark ('Evaluating Judges as Evaluators,' ICML 2025; arXiv:2504.15253), which tests LLM-as-judges as test-time-scaling evaluators across math, code, and instruction-following. He also led FaithEval (ICLR 2025), a benchmark for contextual faithfulness, plus TRAIL on agentic trace error localization. Strong follow for rigorous LLM-evaluation and faithfulness research.

awards: DARPA AIxCC win (Team Atlanta, $4M, DEF CON 33, 2025); USENIX/Facebook Internet Defense Prize (2015)

name: Taesoo Kim
role: Corporate VP, Security & Privacy Team, Samsung Research; Professor, Georgia Tech.
blurb: Taesoo Kim is Corporate VP of Samsung Research's Security & Privacy Team, leading Samsung's AI security R&D on autonomously detecting and patching software vulnerabilities, while also a tenured systems-security professor at Georgia Tech (director of GTS3). He organized and led Team Atlanta — a 40+ person coalition spanning Samsung Research, Georgia Tech, KAIST and POSTECH — which won first place ($4M) at DARPA's AI Cyber Challenge (AIxCC) finals at DEF CON 33 in August 2025, building an AI-driven Cyber Reasoning System that out-detected and out-patched all rivals. He has a deep systems-security publication record (USENIX Security, IEEE S&P, CCS) and a 2015 USENIX/Facebook Internet Defense Prize. A strong follow for the intersection of frontier AI agents and offensive/defensive security.

notable: Fully Autonomous AI Agents Should Not Be Developed
name: Margaret Mitchell
role: Chief Ethics Scientist, Hugging Face.
blurb: Chief Ethics Scientist at Hugging Face and a co-creator of Model Cards, the now-standard model-documentation framework. In 2025 she co-authored the widely cited position paper 'Fully Autonomous AI Agents Should Not Be Developed' (arXiv:2502.02649), arguing risk to people rises with agent autonomy and advocating constrained semi-autonomous systems with human oversight. She also contributed to the CIVICS dataset for evaluating culturally-informed values in LLMs and remains a leading public voice on responsible AI and agent governance (most active on Bluesky). A top follow for AI ethics, documentation standards, and the agent-autonomy debate.

role: Research Scientist; leads the ML & Society team, Hugging Face.
blurb: Research scientist at Hugging Face who leads the ML & Society team, working at the intersection of ML governance and regulatory/technical tooling (also an ACLU affiliate and BigScience co-organizer for the BLOOM/ROOTS effort). He co-authored the INTIMA benchmark for human-AI companionship behavior (arXiv:2508.09998; 31 behaviors, 368 prompts) and the CIVICS culturally-informed-values dataset, plus foundational data-governance work for large-scale language tech. Worth following for AI governance, data documentation/governance, and the emerging companionship-risk research area (most active on Bluesky @yjernite.bsky.social).

talks: LLM Apps: Evaluation (W&B course); RAG++ (W&B course)
name: Ayush Thakur
role: Manager, AI Engineer (Applied AI / LLMOps), Weights & Biases (CoreWeave).
blurb: Weights & Biases (now owned by CoreWeave) is an MLOps/tooling company, not a safety research lab, so there is no dedicated adversarial-ML researcher. The closest substantive figure is Ayush Thakur, who leads open-source LLMOps/eval content for the Weave toolkit and authored W&B's 'LLM Apps: Evaluation' course (with Google AI) and the 'RAG++' course. His work goes deep on safety-relevant evaluation methodology, including LLM-as-a-judge design, bias mitigation (position, verbosity, misinformation biases), and measuring judge-human agreement via Cohen's kappa and Kendall's tau. A Google Developer Expert in ML and the primary technical author behind W&B's eval guidance, rather than a pure devrel/exec voice — but note this is eval tooling, not published safety research.  ⚠️ _[thin research footprint — included for completeness]_

Copenhagen 2020-2024 before joining Lakera in 2024 — the professor title is now stale). He is a genuine, prolific researcher: senior author of 'Gandalf the Red: Adaptive Security for LLMs' (ICML 2025, arXiv 2501.07927), which introduced the D-SEC dynamic security-utility threat model and released a 279k-prompt-attack dataset. In late 2025 he co-authored 'Breaking Agent Backbones' (arXiv 2510.22620) and the b3 agent-security benchmark. Lakera was acquired by Check Point for ~$300M (closed Oct 22, 2025). Strong follow for adaptive LLM defenses, agent security, and red-team benchmark research.

blurb: Confirmed: he left Lakera (Zurich) for Kyutai, the Paris open-science AI lab, in March 2025, where he now works on audio/speech research rather than security. He was a core contributor and co-author on 'Gandalf the Red' (ICML 2025) and designed the original Gandalf prompt-injection game at Lakera (played by 1M+ people), which generated the public attack datasets. His AI-security output is now mostly historical (Gandalf-era) since his current work has shifted away from the security domain. Still a worthwhile follow for prompt-injection/red-teaming context and as a Gandalf co-creator.

name: Kasimir Schulz
role: Director of Security Research, HiddenLayer.
blurb: A leading hands-on AI/ML supply-chain vulnerability researcher and lead author on HiddenLayer's most cited model-security work. He led ShadowLogic (no-code logic backdoors implanted directly in ML computational graphs that persist across fine-tuning), co-led ShadowGenes (signature-based model architecture/genealogy identification, arXiv 2501.11830, ~97.5% TPR over 1,400+ models), and KROP (Knowledge Return-Oriented Prompting jailbreaks). His 2025 work also spans edge-AI device attacks (Wyze Cam) and AI-assistant security research. He specializes in zero-days and supply-chain flaws in AI tooling and is regularly cited in Forbes, BleepingComputer and Dark Reading. No discoverable personal X handle — he appears publicly via @hiddenlayersec. Strong follow for AI model-file/supply-chain attack research.

name: Eoin Wickens
role: Director of Threat Intelligence, HiddenLayer (Synaptic Adversarial Intelligence team).
blurb: Genuine researcher and threat-intel lead behind the ML-model-as-attack-vector thesis (title updated to Director of Threat Intelligence per 2025 sources). He pioneered work on malicious model/pickle-file deserialization attacks — authored the public MaliciousPickle YARA rule and co-authored 'Weaponizing ML Models with Ransomware' (with Tom Bonner, Marta Janus) showing models used to infiltrate enterprise networks. Ex-BlackBerry/Cylance malware reverse engineer; co-authored a book on Cobalt Strike threat intel. In 2025 he led HiddenLayer's annual AI Threat Landscape Report and contributed to AI supply-chain efforts (e.g. OpenSSF Model Signing). Speaks at DEF CON AI Village, LABScon, 44CON. Strong follow for AI supply-chain / model-file threat intelligence.

blurb: Leads HiddenLayer's adversarial-ML research (the Synaptic Adversarial Intelligence / SAI team that Eoin Wickens and Kasimir Schulz sit on). Ex-Distinguished Threat Researcher at BlackBerry and Director of Threat Research at Cylance, he co-authored foundational HiddenLayer work including 'Weaponizing Machine Learning Models with Ransomware' and the firm's adversarial-ML tactics-and-techniques framing. He remains research-active in 2025 (e.g. May 2025 publications) and is a recurring conference voice on the ML supply-chain threat landscape. Strong follow for adversarial-ML and model-file attack research.

Co-authored HiddenLayer's foundational 'Weaponizing Machine Learning Models with Ransomware' (with Tom Bonner and Eoin Wickens) demonstrating model files as malware carriers, contributes to the annual AI Threat Landscape Report, and presented 'Sleeping with one AI open' at LABScon 2023 on ML supply-chain attacks. Worth following for malware-grade reverse-engineering applied to model-file and AI supply-chain threats.

role: Lead AI Threat Researcher, Protect AI (now part of Palo Alto Networks).
blurb: Highly active, hands-on AI vulnerability researcher with 15 years of red-team experience; helps run Protect AI's huntr AI/ML bug-bounty platform. Headline finds: CVE-2023-1177 in MLflow (CVSS 10.0, LFI/RFI account takeover) and two LocalAI flaws — CVE-2024-6983 (RCE, CVSS 8.8) and CVE-2024-7010 (timing-based API-key guessing). He maintains the popular protectai/ai-exploits GitHub repo of responsibly-disclosed AI/ML exploits and blogs at mcinerney.ai. Protect AI was acquired by Palo Alto Networks in 2025 (completed July 22, ~$650-700M) and folded into Prisma AIRS. Strong follow for practical AI/MLOps CVE disclosure and supply-chain exploit research.

name: Paul Kassianik
role: Principal AI Security Researcher, Cisco Foundation AI.
blurb: Active researcher bridging jailbreak research and security-tuned LLMs (came over via Cisco's 2024 acquisition of Robust Intelligence). Co-author of TAP (Tree of Attacks with Pruning), a widely cited automated jailbreak method. He led the early-2025 Cisco/UPenn DeepSeek R1 jailbreak study (100% attack success on HarmBench), cited by multiple legislative bodies. Most notably he led research, technical direction, and pretraining of Foundation-Sec-8B, Cisco Foundation AI's open-weight cybersecurity-specialized LLM (continued pretraining on Llama-3.1-8B), released April 2025. Strong follow for jailbreak techniques, frontier-model security evaluation, and security-domain LLMs.

name: Hyrum Anderson
role: Co-Founder & CEO, Security Superintelligence Labs (ssil.ai); ex-CTO Robust Intelligence / Cisco.
blurb: Long-standing genuine leader in adversarial ML who still publishes despite the exec title. In March 2025 he co-authored (for Cisco) the updated NIST AI 100-2e2025 Adversarial Machine Learning Taxonomy with Apostol Vassilev, Alina Oprea, Xander Davies (UK AISI) and others — a foundational reference. Co-author of TAP and of the 2024 Wiley book 'Not with a Bug, But with a Sticker.' Career spans Chief Scientist at Endgame, Principal Architect of Trustworthy ML at Microsoft, MIT Lincoln Lab and Sandia; co-founder of the CAMLIS conference. Now building Security Superintelligence Labs. Strong follow for adversarial-ML standards and AI-security thought leadership — not a pure exec.

Qian co-founded Patronus in 2023 (with Anand Kannappan, both ex-Meta AI) and drives its research output: Lynx (open-source SOTA hallucination-detection model, arXiv 2407.08488), the HaluBench benchmark, SimpleSafetyTests and FinanceBench. Patronus's 2026 direction is agent stress-testing via simulated 'digital worlds.' Her work is more LLM-eval/safety/reliability than adversarial security per se, but she is a substantive technical co-founder who ships research and open models. Good follow for LLM evaluation, hallucination detection, and agent reliability benchmarks.

blurb: Prompt Security was acquired by SentinelOne (announced Aug 2025, closed Sept 2025, ~$180M) and folded into SentinelOne's Singularity platform for GenAI/agent security. The firm has no standalone academic-style publishing research scientist; its research credibility comes from founders being core OWASP GenAI contributors. The best-qualified named figure is CEO/co-founder Itamar Golan, a core team member of the OWASP Top 10 for LLM Applications (ex-IDF, Check Point, Orca Security). Co-founder & CTO Lior Drihem is also an OWASP LLM contributor. Output is product/blog-driven rather than peer-reviewed — fundamentally an exec/OWASP-contributor, not a pure publishing researcher.  ⚠️ _[thin research footprint — included for completeness]_

blurb: Polyakov remains an active, publishing AI red-team researcher, not a pure exec. He authored the widely-covered 'Universal LLM Jailbreak' work (WIRED) and contributed to the NIST AI RMF. In 2025-26 Adversa AI ran a large-scale frontier red-team study spanning ~100 model entries across 17 vendor families with 24,956 adversarial probes, finding that reasoning modes can act as a protective mechanism against attacks. He publishes 'Towards Trusted AI' weekly threat roundups and authored a hands-on AI red-teaming guide. Strong ongoing research output through his firm.

name: Eugene Neelou
role: Head of AI Security, Wallarm (ex-Co-founder/CTO, Adversa AI).
blurb: Correction: Neelou has LEFT Adversa AI and is now Head of AI Security at Wallarm. He coined the term 'MLSecOps' and authored the MLSecOps Framework, co-authored the OWASP Top 10 for LLM (and for Agents), contributed to early NIST AI RMF versions, and won SANS Researcher of the Year. His major 2025-26 work is leading the A2AS (Agentic AI Runtime Security and Self-Defense) standard/research project, announced Sept 2025, collaborating with researchers from AWS, ByteDance, Cisco, Google, JPMorganChase, Meta and Salesforce — proposing a security layer for AI agents analogous to HTTPS for HTTP. A genuine, active publishing researcher and standards author.

name: Aaron Portnoy
role: Head of Research & Innovation, Mindgard (joined July 2025).
blurb: Deeply technical, hands-on researcher with 20+ years in offensive security. He created/judged the first six Pwn2Own contests, founded ZDI (Zero Day Initiative) and co-founded Exodus Intelligence, and has personally discovered hundreds of 0-days across OpenAI, Cursor, NVIDIA, Microsoft, Google, Amazon, Palo Alto Networks and Adobe. Recent 2025-26 Mindgard work under him includes revealing Sora 2's system prompt via cross-modal experimentation and demonstrating prompt injection into source files of the Cline AI coding agent. Clearly a vuln-disclosing researcher, not an exec.

notable: Bypassing LLM Guardrails: An Empirical Analysis of Evasion Attacks
name: Peter Garraghan
role: Founder & Chief Scientist (also CEO in some sources), Mindgard; Professor, Lancaster University.
blurb: Dr. Peter Garraghan founded Mindgard out of 10+ years of his Lancaster University AI-security research and remains a Professor of Computer Science there. He co-authored 'Bypassing LLM Guardrails: An Empirical Analysis of Evasion Attacks' (arXiv 2504.11168), showing even billion-dollar LLM guardrails can be bypassed with simple techniques — character injection, emojis, context manipulation, instruction fragmentation, and algorithmic adversarial-ML evasion. In 2025-26 Mindgard launched GuardBuster (May 2026), measuring how AI guardrails/gateways perform under realistic agentic attack conditions. A genuine academic researcher who actively publishes and gives security interviews. No widely-used personal X handle found.

name: Ziv Karliner
role: Co-Founder & CTO, Pillar Security.
blurb: Karliner led the team that discovered the 'Rules File Backdoor' attack (disclosed March 2025), a supply-chain technique that hides malicious instructions in AI coding-assistant rule/config files using invisible Unicode characters, making Cursor and GitHub Copilot silently inject backdoors into generated code. It is cataloged as MITRE ATLAS case study AML.CS0041 and drove a real vendor response — GitHub added hidden-Unicode warnings on github.com. He is a hands-on offensive AI-security researcher leading practical vulnerability research, not a pure exec. No clearly-verified personal X handle found.

name: Ariel Fogel
role: Founding Engineer & AI Security Researcher, Pillar Security.
blurb: Fogel led Pillar Security's discovery of 'Poisoned GGUF Templates' — a novel inference-level backdoor where malicious instructions are embedded in the chat template of GGUF model files on Hugging Face, exploiting the gap between the clean template shown in the repo UI and the malicious one that runs locally, evading casual testing and most scanners. Pillar responsibly disclosed to Hugging Face and LM Studio in June 2025 and published in July 2025. He co-authored the Feb 2026 follow-up 'From Discovery to Large-Scale Validation: Chat Template Backdoors Across 18 Models and 4 Engines,' showing sustained output. A working researcher, not an exec.

name: Bar Lanyado
role: Security Researcher, Lasso Security.
blurb: Lanyado is the originator of the 'AI package hallucination' attack class now widely called slopsquatting (the slopsquatting term was coined by PSF's Seth Larson in April 2025; Lanyado documented the underlying technique in 2023-24). He proved it empirically by uploading an empty 'huggingface-cli' package — a name LLMs repeatedly hallucinated — to PyPI, which drew 30,000+ real downloads in three months and was even copied into Alibaba's public docs. His earlier work also surfaced 1,681 exposed Hugging Face API tokens (2023). His thesis was validated at scale by a USENIX Security 2025 study finding ~19.7% of LLM-suggested packages are hallucinated. Genuine, influential research.

The closest named research figure is President & CTO James White, the public voice of CASI and the 'Agentic Warfare' red-teaming methodology (positioned as a weighted alternative to raw Attack Success Rate). CalypsoAI was acquired by F5 (Sept 2025, ~$180M); the work continues as F5 AI Red Team. Contributor to SPLX's open-source Agentic Radar scanner for LLM agentic workflows. Good follow for hands-on LLM red-teaming and jailbreak research.

Tang and Haize made Forbes 30 Under 30 (AI) and the 2025 IA40 list. A CEO who remains research-active. Strong follow for automated red-teaming and LLM-judge/evaluation research.

PhD in algebraic topology, ex-senior data scientist at Elastic (malware model training pipeline); his startup nbhd.ai works on the security and integrity of datasets and the models built on them, and he is a persistent advocate for ML vulnerability disclosure and CVE-style practice for AI. Worth following as the central organizer of public, participatory AI red-teaming.

name: Elham Tabassi

blurb: Tabassi is CTO of NIST's CAISI (the renamed successor to the US AI Safety Institute), and was previously NIST's Chief AI Advisor and head of its AI Innovation Lab. She was the lead author and architect of the NIST AI Risk Management Framework (AI RMF 1.0, January 2023) and oversaw the Generative AI Profile (NIST AI 600-1, July 2024), the most widely adopted voluntary AI governance framework in the US. In 2025-26 her organization launched the AI Agent Standards Initiative (Feb 2026), developing voluntary guidelines for agent identity, authorization, security, and logging, with an AI Agent Interoperability Profile targeted for Q4 2026. She is one of the most influential figures in US federal AI standards and was named to TIME's 100 most influential people in AI.

currentOrg: NIST AI / US AI Safety Institute (now CAISI)
name: Stevie Bergman
org: NIST AI / US AI Safety Institute (now CAISI)
role: Head of Applied Systems, CAISI.
blurb: Bergman leads CAISI's Applied Systems team, which uses multidisciplinary methods to analyze AI systems in real-world application and advance AI measurement science (the scientific study of how to assess AI system properties and impacts). She came to NIST from research roles as a senior research scientist on Google DeepMind's Ethics Research team and Facebook's Responsible AI team, giving her unusually deep applied-research credentials for a standards-body lead. Her team contributes to NIST's AI 800-series publications and to CAISI's 2025-26 push on agentic AI evaluation and measurement. She has publicly emphasized equipping AI adopters to evaluate and choose systems for their specific use cases.

blurb: Vassilev is the NIST lead author of 'Adversarial Machine Learning: A Taxonomy and Terminology of Attacks and Mitigations' (NIST AI 100-2), the foundational reference taxonomy for AML attacks (evasion, poisoning, privacy, and GenAI abuse) and their mitigations. The taxonomy underpins much downstream AI-security standards and threat-modeling work; an expanded edition with a larger GenAI section was released subsequently. It was co-authored with Alina Oprea (Northeastern) and others. He supervises a research team in NIST's Computer Security Division.

blurb: The most influential technical-alignment researcher of the RLHF era: co-author of 'Deep Reinforcement Learning from Human Preferences' (2017), which established RLHF; originator of iterated amplification and (with Geoffrey Irving) AI safety via debate; and author of the Eliciting Latent Knowledge (ELK) agenda at ARC, which he founded after leading OpenAI's language-model alignment team. Joined the US AI Safety Institute in April 2024 as Head of AI Safety — designing frontier-model evaluations for national-security-relevant capabilities and guidance on risk mitigations — through the institute's rebranding as CAISI. Worth following as the intellectual root of much of modern alignment research, now embedded in the US government's evaluation apparatus.

name: Wael William Diab
role: Chair.
blurb: Diab is the founding and continuing Chair of ISO/IEC JTC 1/SC 42, the central global venue for AI standardization. SC 42 under his leadership produced ISO/IEC 42001 (the world's first AI management-system standard), ISO/IEC 23894 (AI risk management), ISO/IEC 22989 (concepts/terminology), and the TR 24028 trustworthiness series. He is a convener and strategist rather than a research author, coordinating hundreds of national-body experts across foundational standards, use cases, trustworthiness, and governance. Through 2025-26 SC 42 has remained the reference point harmonizing AI governance with the EU AI Act, NIST AI RMF, and national regimes. Thin on personal research output, but his standards-authorship and committee leadership are a substantial contribution.  ⚠️ _[thin research footprint — included for completeness]_

blurb: Cotton convenes SC 42's Working Group 1 on Foundational Standards, the group directly responsible for ISO/IEC 42001 (AI management systems), ISO/IEC 22989 (AI concepts and terminology), and ISO/IEC 23053 (ML framework). He has led WG 1 since the work began in July 2018, coordinating over 400 international experts, and received a 2023 IEC 1906 Award for this contribution. As the technical lead behind 42001, he is one of the people most directly responsible for the structure of the now widely certified AI management-system standard. His role is convening and technical editing rather than independent research, but the foundational AI standards he shepherded are heavily load-bearing for global AI governance and conformity assessment.  ⚠️ _[thin research footprint — included for completeness]_

He co-led the creation of 'Ethically Aligned Design,' the foundational document that seeded the IEEE P7000-series of ethics standards (covering transparency, data privacy, algorithmic bias, and value-based design). Unlike most pure standards conveners he has a substantial robotics/AI research record, bridging technical AI and ethics standardization. He remains an active voice on AI ethics, governance, and the responsible design of autonomous systems through 2025-26.

He chairs the MLCommons AI Risk & Reliability working group, which produced the AILuminate benchmark for measuring AI product risk and reliability across 12 hazard categories. Through 2025-26 he has driven AILuminate's expansion to new languages (English, French, Chinese, Hindi) and a NASSCOM partnership to bring it to South Asia, and is extending it toward multimodal and agentic systems. His contribution is org-building and benchmark standardization more than individual research papers.  ⚠️ _[thin research footprint — included for completeness]_

blurb: Huang co-chairs the Cloud Security Alliance's AI Controls Framework working group (since November 2023) and is a prolific author on generative and agentic AI security. He chairs the OpenSSF AI/ML working group and was a lead author of the model-signing effort, shipping model-signing v1.0 in April 2025 — bringing Sigstore-based tamper-resistant signatures to ML models of any format and size. This fed directly into CoSAI's 'Signing ML Artifacts' workstream for tamper-proof ML metadata, done with NVIDIA, HiddenLayer, and OpenSSF, and he contributes to GUAC for supply-chain provenance. A hands-on engineer and standards author whose model-signing work is foundational infrastructure for ML supply-chain integrity.

She is a self-described technology pioneer and AI scientist with a commercial-deployment background — early real-time AI recommendation systems, mobile music/video streaming, connected vehicles — and now advises governments and business leaders on digital transformation. Her profile is that of a convener, advisor and governance chair rather than a publishing safety researcher, so her independent research output is thin; she is most useful to follow as a lead voice in GPAI's multistakeholder governance.  ⚠️ _[thin research footprint — included for completeness]_

He chairs the International AI Safety Report, the 30-country/UN/EU/OECD scientific assessment: the first full edition launched January 29, 2025, a 'Key Update' on reasoning-model controllability followed in October 2025, and the second full edition published February 3, 2026 ahead of the AI Impact Summit. This makes him the single most authoritative convening scientist on AI safety, bridging frontier technical research and intergovernmental risk assessment. Emphatically not thin — he is both an active researcher and the world's premier AI-safety report author.

She is a better current representative of the Foundation than Gabriel Chua, who authored the multilingual RabakBench and LionGuard 2 safety benchmarks at GovTech but has since moved to OpenAI (@gabrielchua_). McGovern Foundation), and has published its multi-level definitional and data-collection methodology. He co-founded UNESCO's International Research Centre on AI (IRCAI) and is CEO of Quintelligence. His 2025-26 output centers on post-deployment monitoring and incident analysis for AI agents. A genuine researcher with both technical and governance contributions.

She is a central architect and steward of the OECD AI Principles — the first intergovernmental AI standard — and coordinated their 2024 update to address generative AI and large language models and to sharpen definitions for national implementation. Her division also delivers the OECD AI Incidents Monitor (AIM) framework and tooling. In 2025-26 she has focused on putting the updated Principles into action regionally. As a policy lead, her worthy contribution is authorship and stewardship of globally-adopted AI governance standards rather than independent technical research; the @karinov handle could not be independently confirmed.  ⚠️ _[thin research footprint — included for completeness]_

Key 2025-26 contributions include AgentHarm (ICLR 2025 benchmark for agent harmfulness/jailbreakability), STACK (staged attacks defeating defense-in-depth pipelines at ~71% ASR on catastrophic-misuse data), Deep Ignorance (pretraining-data filtering for tamper-resistant open-weight safeguards), and work on agent vulnerability to indirect prompt injection. He is one of the most prominent government-side voices on adversarial testing, jailbreak robustness, and agent safety. X handle @alxndrdavies and website xanderdavies.com both verified.

His 2025 work covers sandbagging and exploration hacking (deliberate underperformance), control evaluations for increasingly autonomous agents, and field-building on what realistic control looks like for near-superhuman systems. His Redwood blog is a key venue for control thinking. Follow for the control research program.

A distinctive, mathematically rigorous interpretability program. Follow for the SLT-for-safety agenda.

He provides the theoretical foundations behind Timaeus's developmental-interpretability program, grounding the Local Learning Coefficient and degeneracy-of-the-loss-landscape analyses used to track how internal structure emerges during training. Follow for SLT foundations and their safety applications.

Follow for China's leading adversarial-ML and robust-generative-model research. (X is blocked in China.)

name: Minlie Huang
role: Professor of CS, Tsinghua; leads the CoAI group.
blurb: Leads Tsinghua's CoAI group, a major source of LLM safety-alignment research. His group built SafetyBench (ACL 2024, 11k+ multiple-choice safety questions), Agent-SafetyBench (2025) for LLM-agent safety, and AISafetyLab (2025), a comprehensive framework covering safety attacks, defenses, and evaluation, alongside safe-RLHF-style alignment and fine-tuning-robustness work (e.g., why guardrails collapse after fine-tuning). Follow for one of the most prolific non-Western LLM safety-alignment and benchmarking labs.

Ex-IBM Research AI VP and JD.com CTO; a convener of the 2025 'Shanghai Consensus on AI Safety' discussions with Andrew Yao, Stuart Russell, and Yoshua Bengio, and a panelist at the WAIC 2025 AI Safety and Governance Forum. Worth following as the most senior technical leader of China's official AI-safety research agenda. (X is blocked in China.)

notable: DecodingTrust

blurb: A leading trustworthy-ML researcher who led DecodingTrust (NeurIPS 2023 best benchmark) comprehensively assessing GPT-model trustworthiness, plus extensive work on certified robustness, backdoor attacks, and AI risk taxonomies (AIR-Bench). She co-founded Virtue AI and, as of 2025-26, joined Meta Superintelligence Labs while remaining an academic. Follow for the most systematic trustworthiness-evaluation research.

currentOrg: University of Toronto / Schwartz Reisman Institute
name: Geoffrey Hinton
org: University of Toronto / Schwartz Reisman Institute
role: University Professor Emeritus, University of Toronto.
blurb: Turing Award and 2024 Nobel laureate who left Google in 2023 to speak freely about existential risk from advanced AI. While his foundational research is on deep learning (backprop, Boltzmann machines), he is now one of the most prominent academic voices warning about loss-of-control and superintelligence risk. Follow for the highest-profile scientific endorsement of taking catastrophic AI risk seriously.

notable: Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT (ROME); Mass-Editing Memory in a Transformer (MEMIT); GAN Dissection

blurb: Interpretability researcher behind the model-editing lineage: ROME ('Locating and Editing Factual Associations in GPT'), MEMIT (mass-editing memory in a transformer), and earlier GAN Dissection. He directs the NSF-funded National Deep Inference Fabric (NDIF), which gives researchers white-box access to frontier-scale open models via the nnsight library — critical shared infrastructure for interpretability and safety research — and spoke at the NeurIPS 2024 SATA (Safe & Trustworthy Agents) workshop. Worth following for concrete, causal methods to locate and edit what models know.
